critic_raw:
Assessor A: The problem is straightforward: the metric used (AUC) measures something different from what the business needs to know (top-2% selection quality). This is not a minor issue — the entire evaluation is structured around the wrong primary metric for the deployment decision. Verdict: empirical_test_agreed.

Assessor B: The data science team argues AUC is threshold-independent and robust. For a development and model-selection context, this is correct. But the model is being proposed for a production workflow change. That decision should be supported by production-relevant metrics. AUC is appropriate for building the model; precision@2% is appropriate for deploying it. Verdict: empirical_test_agreed.

Assessor C: Three evaluation gaps: (1) no capacity-constrained metric, (2) no top-k list quality metric, (3) no intervention outcome metric. The model may still be better than the incumbent — but the evaluation provided does not prove it for the operational use case. Verdict: empirical_test_agreed.

Synthesis: All three assessors vote empirical_test_agreed. All three identify the same metric alignment issues. Compute top-2% metrics to resolve.
adjudication_raw: Unanimous empirical_test_agreed across three assessors. Consistent issue identification across all three runs of this case.

empirical_test.measure: top-2% precision and lift on held-out test month
empirical_test.success_criterion: new model shows higher precision and lift at the 2% cutoff than the incumbent logistic model
empirical_test.failure_criterion: top-2% performance metrics are not improved despite higher global AUC
